question_frequency.rationale: The Pair posed didactic A/B options at virtually every meaningful decision point across multiple problems — partition condition, median extraction, swap rationale, search bounds, feasibility implementations, loop template, ceiling formula, last-burst framing, state definition, fill order, etc. Easily a dozen+ structured questions.

question_quality.rationale: Choices consistently target the crux: cross-array vs per-array validity, parity branch vs always-average, correctness vs perf rationale for the swap, leftmost-true vs rightmost-false template, ceiling vs floor division, last-burst vs first-burst, open vs closed interval state, length-major vs row-major fill order. These are exactly the conceptual forks that distinguish understanding from memorization.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Tone is warm, concise, and engaged; the Pair acknowledges its own mistakes (Cutting Ribbons template description, bad expected test values) honestly. Flow is mostly natural, though the model occasionally generates spurious 'User:' turns inside its own messages (Koko, Strange Printer), which is awkward and would feel jarring in a real session.

effectiveness.rationale: Final code for all four solved problems (median, split array, Koko, ribbons, balloons, strange printer) is correct and verified — including a brute-force cross-check on Burst Balloons. Goes well beyond the baseline, which only solved the original problem; experiment delivers an entire curriculum of correct solutions.

friction.rationale: Compared to baseline's 3 turns/116s on a single problem, the experiment ran 18 turns/1110s but covered six problems with substantial pedagogical payoff per problem. The teaching gain is clearly proportional to the extra length, though some self-inflicted friction from incorrect test expectations and the repeated state-definition question prevent a 5.
